coredata.dc:title: Preparation and investigation of the heat transfer properties of a novel nanofluid based on graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ettefaghi, Ehsanollah
Ghobadian, Barat
Rashidi, Alimorad
Najafi, G.
Khoshtaghaza, Mohammad Hadi
Pourhashem, Sepideh
coredata.dc:description: In the present research, the biocompatible graphene quantum dots were used to prepare heat transfer nanofluids for the first time. Graphene quantum dots are a new generation of carbon nanoparticles capable of being synthesized from biodegradable and non-toxic resources. In this research, graphene quantum dots were produced by hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea as precursor. Nanofluid samples were synthesized based on car radiator coolant and graphene quantum dots at very low concentrations including 100, 200, 500, and 1000ppm. One of the advantages of the graphene quantum dots was their long-term stability within the base fluid without using any chemical surfactants. In order to investigate the nanofluids’ heat transfer properties, thermal conductivity (k) and convection heat transfer (h) coefficients of the samples were measured. The results for 100ppm concentration indicated 5.2% and 17% improvement of k and h, compared to the base fluid, respectively. Further, the results demonstrated that the nanofluids containing graphene quantum dots had better stability and performance at low concentrations. Thus, it can be concluded that heat transfer nanofluids based on graphene quantum dots not only reduce probable environmental risks, but also are economically appropriate options for industrial applications; leading to reducing the energy consumption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Biodegradable nanoparticles
Nanofluid
Car radiator coolant
Thermal properties
Energy conservation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots and functionalized graphene quantum dots
Characterization of graphene quantum dots and functionalized graphene quantum dots
Preparing the car radiator coolant nanofluids based on functionalized graphene quantum dots
Characterizing graphene quantum dots and functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flowchart of the steps of conducting the experiments.
XRD patterns of GQDs and FGQDs.
FTIR spectra of GQDs and FGQDs.
(a) TEM image and (b) photoluminescence spectra of GQDs.
(a) TEM image and (b) particle size distribution of FGQDs.
Zeta potential of the CRC-FGQDs nanofluid containing 500 ppm of FGQDs.
Validation of experimental set-up as a function of Reynolds number for case of deionized water.
The trend of changes for convection heat transfer coefficient relative to the Reynolds number in different concentration of the nanoparticles.
Comparison of Nusselt number from Gnielinski model and CRC-FGQDs nanofluid.
The specification of chemical compounds used in this research.
The effect of FGQDs concentration on thermal conductivity of nanofluids.
Convection heat transfer coefficient in 100 ppm concentration.

head.ce:title: Preparation and investigation of the heat transfer properties of a novel nanofluid based on graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical & Biosystems Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-336, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Center, Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI), P.O. Box: 14665-1998, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present research, the biocompatible graphene quantum dots were used to prepare heat transfer nanofluids for the first time. Graphene quantum dots are a new generation of carbon nanoparticles capable of being synthesized from biodegradable and non-toxic resources. In this research, graphene quantum dots were produced by hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea as precursor. Nanofluid samples were synthesized based on car radiator coolant and graphene quantum dots at very low concentrations including 100, 200, 500, and 1000ppm. One of the advantages of the graphene quantum dots was their long-term stability within the base fluid without using any chemical surfactants. In order to investigate the nanofluids’ heat transfer properties, thermal conductivity (k) and convection heat transfer (h) coefficients of the samples were measured. The results for 100ppm concentration indicated 5.2% and 17% improvement of k and h, compared to the base fluid, respectively. Further, the results demonstrated that the nanofluids containing graphene quantum dots had better stability and performance at low concentrations. Thus, it can be concluded that heat transfer nanofluids based on graphene quantum dots not only reduce probable environmental risks, but also are economically appropriate options for industrial applications; leading to reducing the energy consumption.

ce:section.ce:para: These days, the issue of energy has become a key issue in the world. High and growing costs of fossil fuels and concerns about the problem of depletion of these non-renewable resources have made the owners of large industries make great efforts to find solutions to the reduction of energy consumption and improve its efficiency. Advanced materials and nanomaterials have found applications in the area of energy including the sectors of generation, storage, and saving. For example, in the sector of energy saving one can mention the issues of nanofluids in which reduction in the volume of heat exchanger and also in the consumption of energy. Although significant progress has been achieved in the field of nanofluids, there are still some problems. Actually, development of nanotechnology and its various applications have caused inevitable interactions between nanomaterials and the environment. Most of the studies have indicated the positive effects of nanoparticles on the physicochemical function of different systems and; on the other hand, the concerns resulted from the emission of different metal and metal oxide nanoparticles in the environment are expressed [1]. In fact, since common metal and metal oxide nanoparticles can remain in different environments for a long time without any physicochemical changes [2], there are many concerns on their potential risks for human health as well as the environment [3]. Hristozov and Malsch [4] studied the hazards of engineered nanoparticles for the environment and human health. Also, Joo and Zhao [5] reviewed the factors that affect the fate, transformation and toxicity of metal oxide nanoparticles.

Nanotechnology is making rapid progress and has entered many scientific fields. In this regard, one of the most applicable fields of nanotechnology is the improvement of fluids’ heat transfer properties [6]. The heat-transfer processes have an effective role in most of the industries, represented in the air conditioning, energy generation, solar collector and chemical processors. For instance, Balaji et al. [7] used nanofluids to improve performance of air conditioning systems. Li et al. [8] employed to improve efficiency of solar collectors and increase the energy generation rate. Also, Anin Vincely and Natarajan [9] conducted experimental studies on the performance of flat plate collectors using graphene oxide-containing nanofluids. They dispersed graphene oxide nanoparticles in deionized water at three different concentrations and, then, measured thermal properties of the produced nanofluids. The results demonstrated that use of nanofluids led to an increase of 7.3% in efficiency of the collectors. Sonawane et al. [10] used Al2O3-Fuel nanofluids to improve performance of aviation turbines.

So far, researchers have been attempting to produce stable nanofluids with high capability of heat transfer based on different nanoparticles. Sarsam et al. [11] investigated effect of various chemical surfactants, including sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), and gum Arabic (GA), on the stabilization of the graphene nanoplatelets in water. They used ultrasonic to homogenize the nanofluid. The obtained results showed that the sample containing SDBS surfactant with the ratio of 1:1 and ultrasonic duration of 60min had good stability for 60days. Leela Vinodhan et al. [12] produced a nanofluid based on water and copper oxide (CuO) nanoparticles and, then, investigated its heat transfer performance in a U-shaped tube. The results showed that the nanofluid’ heat transfer rate and the Nusselt number were higher than those of pure water. Also, Hemmat et al. [13] experimentally investigated thermophysical properties of the nanofluid based on water and silver (Ag) nanoparticles. Although they did not use surfactants to stabilize the nanoparticles within the base fluid, the sample underwent ultrasonic waves with power of 400W for 3h. The obtained results indicated the 11.8% increase in the Nusselt number at volume concentration of 1%. Imani-Mofrad et al. [14] applied the nanofluid based on water and zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles to improve thermal performance of a wet cooling tower. They added the nanoparticles to water at concentration of 0.02–0.1wt%, the results of which indicated the improved performance of the wet cooling tower using the nanofluid. In another study, Umer Ilyas et al. [15] investigated stability and thermal properties of the nanofluids based on thermal oil and alumina (Al2O3) nanoparticles in advanced cooling systems. They used oleic acid to stabilize alumina nanoparticles within the base oil. Furthermore, Ettefaghi et al. [16] studied thermal and rheological properties of the nanofluids based on engine oil and various carbon nanoparticles, including carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon nanoball, and fullerene. They also used probe ultrasonic to stabilize the nanoparticles within the base fluid, the results of which indicated an increase of 18% in thermal conductivity coefficient of the carbon nanoball particles-containing nanofluid. Also, few studies have been conducted on the car radiator coolant (CRC) nanofluids based on different nanoparticles. Muhammad Ali et al. [17] used zinc oxide nanoparticles to improve performance of car radiator. They added the nanoparticles to water at concentrations of 0.01, 0.08, 0.2 and 0.3vol% and, then, investigated the radiator’s heat transfer performance using the produced nanofluids. The obtained results indicated improvement of 46% in the heat transfer coefficient for the nanofluid containing 0.2% volumetric concentration nanoparticles compared to the base fluid. In another work, Sandhya et al. [18] added titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles at different concentrations to a mixture of water and ethylene glycol; afterwards, the convection heat transfer ability of the nanofluids was measured on a car radiator, the results of which indicated an increase of 37% in the heat transfer rate using the nanofluids. Elias et al. [19] studied the thermo-physical properties of Al2O3 nanoparticles suspended in car radiator coolant; thermal conductivity, viscosity, density, and specific heat have been measured at different volume concentrations (i.e. 0–1vol%) of nanoparticles and various temperature ranges (i.e. from 10°C to 50°C). They found that thermal conductivity, viscosity, and density of the nanofluid have increased with increasing the concentration of nanoparticles. However, specific heat of nanofluid was found to be decreased with the increase of nanoparticle volume concentrations. Also, the highest thermal conductivity enhancement was found to be 8.30% for 1vol% of Al2O3–RC nanofluid. However, in general, the use of metal and metal oxide nanoparticles at high concentrations as well as chemical surfactants to stabilize the nanoparticles would have negative effects on the human health and environment. Besides, they would damage the mechanical systems, increase the costs and become non-cost-effective for industrial applications. Therefore, in the present research, it has been attempted to use biocompatible graphene quantum dots (GQDs), instead of common metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, to prepare high quality and cost-effective heat transfer nanofluids.

Graphene quantum dots have currently found increasing attention in wide range of biological applications [20] due to their unique properties and low cost of production. GQDs consist from single to ten-layered graphene sheets with lateral dimensions smaller than 100nm with zero dimensions (0D) [21]. Further, GQDs provide special advantages such as low cytotoxicity, excellent solubility [22], good surface grafting properties, large surface area, robust chemical inertness, tunable band gap, quantum confinement and edge effects [23]. Therefore, GQDs have been extensively used in different fields. For instance, Li et al. [24] have reviewed the recent progress in GQD-based optoelectronics and energy devices, indicating the wide potential application of GQDs for development of communication and energy functional devices. Also, Zhang et al. [25] have considered the achievements obtained in energy and charge transfer devices such as fuel cells and light-emitting diodes via using GQDs. Zhu et al. [26] have used GQDs as biocompatible and low toxicity material for bioimaging. Chen et al. [27] have investigated the potential of GQDs for sensors with promise selectivity and response. However, to the best of our knowledge, the potential of GQDs for enhancing the properties of heat transfer nanofluids has not been explored. Hence, in this research, the properties of GQDs as additive in nanofluids have been investigated.

A variety of methods have been reported for synthesizing GQDs which can be divided to two main categories including “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods. In the “top-down” method, the carbonaceous materials are cut into graphene dots by hydrothermal treatment, chemical or electrochemical oxidation, electron beam, and microwave process [28]. In this regard, Lin and Zhang [29] have used an effective method based on intercalation and exfoliation of carbon nanotubes and graphite for synthesizing GQDs via “top-down” procedure. Moreover, Shinde and Pillai [30] have prepared GQDs from multiwall carbon nanotube by electrochemical method. However, the most important disadvantage of “top-down” method is expensive starting materials and extremely low reaction yields [31]. On the other hand, on the “bottom-up” method, GQDs are synthesized from organic compounds [32]. The “bottom-up” methods are efficient routes to produce fluorescent quantum dots in large scale [28]. Therefore, in this research, GQDs are synthesized via “bottom-up” method with high production yield.

The main aim of the present research is to introduce nanofluids that are environmentally friendly and cost-effective with high stability and suitable heat transfer capability which leads to energy conservation. Thus, GQDs were selected for the first time and their effects as nano additive on the properties of nanofluids were investigated. Also, the prepared GQDs were carbonized, activated, and functionalized. It should be mentioned that the main aim of activation process is to increase the specific surface area, enrich the pore structure, and enhance thermal stability and conductivity. Since the content of nitrogen and oxygen atoms would be reduced during carbonization and activation procedures, the products of activation method are functionalized (FGQDs) to improve their dispersibility in solvent [33]. In this regard, the synthesis of GQDs, the production of stable CRC-GQDs nanofluids, and heat transfer properties of the samples were studied.

As mentioned in Table 1 , all chemicals used in this research were purchased from Sigma Aldrich or Merck and these chemicals were used without further purification. The steps of conducting the experiments are shown in Fig.1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots and functionalized graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: In this research, citric acid and urea were used as the precursor for the synthesis of GQDs. Accordingly, 0.21g citric acid and 0.18g urea were dissolved properly in 5mL deionized water. Then, the resulting solution was transferred to the Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave underwent hydrothermal process in an electrical furnace at 160°C for 4h. After the completion of the reaction time and cooling the autoclave body to the ambient temperature, the final dark blue solution which contains GQDs was dried at 80°C. The obtained GQDs can be easily dispersed in DI water.

Afterwards, a thermal treatment process was performed in order to increase the specific surface area and activate the synthesized GQDs. The synthesized GQDs in the previous step were carbonized under inert gas atmosphere at 700°C for 1h to remove non-carbon elements. Then, the products of carbonization process were activated via chemical method using potassium hydroxide (KOH) precursor. For this purpose, the weight ratio of KOH to nanoparticles was considered as 2:1. Then, the homogeneous mixture of KOH and nanoparticles underwent thermal operations under inert gas atmosphere at 800°C for 1h. The resulting sample was washed with DI water several times to obtain pH 7. Finally, the activated GQDs were dried at 100°C. Since the obtained product lacked the functional groups and could not be dispersed in solvents, the acid nitric steam method was used for surface modification and oxidation of the carbonic materials in order to create functional groups on carbon nanostructures and increase their dispersibility. Then, the final product was functionalized, which was named FGQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of graphene quantum dots and functionalized graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of GQDs and FGQDs were recorded by (Philips PW 3710, 30KV, 35mA, Cu Kα). Fourier transforms infrared spectra (FTIR, Perkin Elmer Spectrum-GX) were obtained over wavelength range of 400–4000cm−1. The morphology of samples was considered by high transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Zeiss, EM900). The size distribution of the GQDs and FGQDs was also considered by dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique. The photoluminescence spectrum of the GQDs was recorded by Fluorescent spectrometer (VARIAN, CARY ECLIPSE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparing the car radiator coolant nanofluids based on functionalized graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The car radiator coolant (CRC) used in vehicles, as a heat transfer fluid widely used in industries, was selected for synthesizing the nanofluids. The radiator coolant was made by mixing distilled water and Caspian Antifreeze (Fouman chimie, under license of Caspian International Ltd. England) at the ratio of 1:1. The CRC-FGQDs nanofluid samples were synthesized at four different nanoparticle concentrations, including 100, 200, 500, and 1000ppm. The nanoparticles were stabilized using merely the bath ultrasonic (P120h, Elmasonic, Germany) within the base fluid and no chemical surfactant was used. A certain amount of FGQD nanoparticles was added to the base fluid and then, the sample container was put in the bath ultrasonic at the frequency of 37KHz for 5min.

The thermal conductivity (k) and convection heat transfer (h) coefficients were investigated as the main characteristics of the fluids’ heat transfer capability. Thermal conductivity of the nanofluid samples and base fluid were measured using KD2-Pro (Decagon devices, USA) at 25°C. Moreover, in order to measure the convection heat transfer coefficient of the samples, the laboratory system designed for this purpose was used. As shown in Fig. 2 , this system consisted of test section, pump, fluid reservoir, shell-tube exchanger, and circulator. The test component was composed of a straight copper pipe with the inner diameter of 11.42mm and length of 1m. The copper pipe was covered by an element heated by AC current and a ceramic insulation with the thickness of 150mm which can prevent heat loss. Temperature of the tube surface was measured using 5 thermocouples of K type and the input and output temperatures of the fluid mass were measured by 2 other thermocouples. The flow rate could be adjusted at 1.15–6min−1. Besides, the fluid was cooled using a shell-tube exchanger and circulator. Finally, the convection heat transfer coefficient was calculated using the following equations:
(1)
h ( x ) =
q
″
T
s
( x ) -
T
m
( x )
(2)
q =
m
̇
·
C
p
(
T
out
-
T
in
)
where
T
s
is the average temperature of pipe wall,
T
m
is the fluid temperature,
C
p
is the thermal capacity,
m
̇
is the fluid mass flow rate,
T
out
and
T
in
are fluid-copper pipe input and output temperatures, respectively. Also,
q
″
is the constant thermal flux obtained by dividing
q
thermal flux by the area surrounding the copper pipe.

In general, there are many resources for uncertainty analysis including the tools of measurement, data recording, and data analysis in the experimental works. The set-up uncertainty used in this research has been previously calculated by Askari et al [34]. Who have reported the value of set-up uncertainty equal to nearly 5.9% based on their precise calculations.

In the present section, the obtained results of preparation and characterization of the GQDs and nanofluid samples are discussed in detail. Also, the final results of thermal conductivity and convection heat transfer coefficients are investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizing graphene quantum dots and functionalized graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The structure of the GQDs and FGQDs was characterized by XRD. As Fig. 3 shows, the characteristic peak of graphite at 2θ=27° corresponding to (002) plane with 0.33nm layer spacing has appeared in the XRD pattern of GQDs. However, in the case of FGQDs, the (002) diffraction peak has occurred at 2θ=24° with d spacing of 0.37nm. Compared to GQDs, the increase of d spacing for FGQDs can be attributed to decoration of functional groups on graphene dots [35] which is further considered via FTIR method.

The FTIR spectra of GQDs and FGQDs are shown in Fig. 4 . In the case of GQDs, the broad absorption peak observed in the 3100–3500cm−1 implied the presence of hydroxyl (OH) groups. Further, the NH tensile absorption peak was also overlapping in this region. The absorption characteristic of CO, CN, and CH2 appeared at 1403, 1169, and 1323cm−1, respectively. Besides, the peaks observed at 1575cm−1 and 894cm−1 resulted from the tensile absorption related to CC. For FGQDs, the broad absorption peak observed at 3200–3500cm−1 is related to hydroxyl groups and the peak observed at 1608cm−1 can be attributed to the aromatic CC bonds, indicating the carbonic structure. Further, the absorption peaks observed at 1716 and 1384cm−1 are assigned to the CO and CH3 bonds, respectively. The absorption peak observed at 1218cm−1 resulted from the presence of CH and NH bonds [36]. Therefore, the FTIR results of GQDs and FGQDs show the presence of functional groups in their structure, leading to their solubility in water.

The TEM image and the photoluminescence spectra of GQDs are shown in Fig.5 (a) and (b), respectively. The TEM image shows that the GQDs are dispersed uniformly and their lateral size is less than3 nm. Besides, According to Fig.5(b), the synthesized GQDs exhibit photoluminescence behavior and they can emit strong blue photoluminescence under UV excitation with 365nm wavelength, indicating formation of quantum dots.

Fig. 6 shows the TEM image and DLS results of the synthesized FGQDs. As can be seen, the FGQDs have dot morphology with the mean size of about 1–3nm. As results show, the size of carbon nanoparticles has not changed significantly during thermal treatments and functionalization process.

Investigations showed that long-term and good stability is not commonly resulted from the nanoparticles in the base fluid; in fact, due to the attractive forces, nanoparticles are agglomerated and deposited within the fluid after a while, which spoils the idea of nanofluids. Although several factors affect stability of the nanofluids, size of nanoparticles is known as one of the most important factors. Based on the main dynamic equation, the particle’s deposition velocity in the stable state within the fluid is in accordance with Eq. (3), where r, η,
ρ
,
ρ
′
, and g indicate particle’s radius, fluid’s dynamic viscosity, particle’s density, fluid’s density, and gravity acceleration, respectively [37]. According to Eq. (3), by reducing the particle size, the deposition velocity is reduced by second power; thus, the GQDs, due to their very small size, have much better stability than other nanoparticles. Moreover, the Brownian motion of the nanoparticles, which is increased by reducing the particle size, facilitates the fluid stability.
(3)
V =
2
r
2
9 η
( ρ - ρ ′ ) g

So far, several methods have been investigated for improving the stability of nanoparticles in the base fluid. The use of chemical surfactants is one of the most common approaches. Although studies on this field have led to good results, it should be noted that the use of chemical surfactants may cause corrosion and damage on mechanical surfaces. The nanofluids synthesized in the present research had very good stability due to the presence of functional groups on the surfaces of FGQDs as well as their very small size. The dynamic and static stability of the samples was investigated. Accordingly, first, the zeta potential test was performed on the 500ppm sample which is shown in Fig. 7 .As can be seen, value of the zeta potential was −28mv, indicating good stability of the CRC-FGQDs nanofluid. Besides, its negative value implied the presence of amine groups that contributed to the nanofluid’s stability.

In the next step, in order to investigate the static stability of the synthesized nanofluids, the samples were poured into transparent glass containers and kept in a fully static place for 6months. The changes were examined and recorded visually. Investigating the samples after 6months indicates that no changes and no nanoparticles deposition have occurred. Another important point is that due to the low concentration and high stability of the nanoparticles, the rheological properties of the base fluid will not change considerably, which can prevent damage and dysfunction of the mechanical systems.

Also, it should be noted that in this research, the container was placed in the bath ultrasonic with the frequency of 37kHz at the ambient temperature only for 5min in order to stabilize the quantum dots within the base fluid and no chemical surfactant or other additive was used. Thus, it can be said that the GQDs-containing nanofluids can be an excellent choice for industrial applications due to their very high stability.

According to the studies, various factors including size, material, shape, and concentration of nanoparticles, temperature, type of base fluid, type of flow regime (laminar or turbulent), compounds added to the nanofluid, and many other factors are effective in characterizing the nanofluids and determining their heat transfer capability. In this regard, some studies have expressed that the reduction in heat transfer capability of the nanofluids is caused by the reduction in the particles’ size due to the high tendency of fine particles to agglomeration. However, the idea indicating that the reduced particle size results in the increased heat transfer capability is more acceptable and supported by the two mechanisms of the theory, namely the Brownian motion of nanoparticles and fluid stratification around the particles; because as mentioned earlier, the decrease of nanoparticles size leads to the increased Brownian motion and specific surface area [38]. These cases can be observed in the CRC-FGQDs nanofluids synthesized in the present research due to the very small size of the FGQD nanoparticles and very good stability of the nanofluids.

Thermal conductivity of the base fluid and CRC-FGQDs samples was measured at 4 concentrations at the ambient temperature (25°C) using KD2-Pro (Decagon devices, USA) device; the results of which are shown in Table 2 . As can be seen, the thermal conductivity coefficient of the nanofluid samples was improved by nearly 5–7% compared to the base fluid. Besides, the maximum improvement of the thermal conductivity coefficient compared to the based fluid was 7.1%, which was related to the 500-ppm sample. Here, there are two notable points: first, by increasing the nanoparticles’ concentration, the ascending trend of thermal conductivity coefficient was stopped, which could be caused by the particles’ agglomeration and descending their performance; thus, it can be concluded that the GQDs-containing nanofluids have better performance at low concentrations. Second, in the present research compared to other works, the improvement rate of the thermal conductivity of the GQDs-containing nanofluids was considerable and cost-effective due to very low concentration of nanoparticles; since the increase of nanoparticles concentration would lead to the increased production costs.

Another analysis that should be mentioned here is that the phenomenon of nanoparticles clustering is one of the most important factors in improvement of the nanofluids’ thermal conductivity coefficient. Since the use of nanoparticles with small size and low concentration leads to reduction in the nanoparticles’ clustering, the use of FGQD nanoparticles with size of 1–3nm has resulted in a relatively slight increase in the thermal conductivity of the samples in the present research.

In an internal combustion engine, temperature of the gases at the moment of ignition is increased up to 2000°C; thus, in order to prevent damage to the engine components, it is necessary to dispose 30–40% of the generated heat to the outside environment. The cooling system in the vehicle (radiator system) plays an important role in this regard. One of the methods for improving the function of cooling system in vehicles is to use the fluids with high thermal capacity and high heat transfer capability. The radiator fluid used in the vehicles is usually a mixture of water and ethylene glycol with some additives; thus, it can be improved by adding nanoparticles to the radiator fluid without applying any mechanical changes to the radiator design.

Although the thermal conductivity coefficient is one of the major heat transfer characteristics, making appropriate decisions on the nanofluids’ heat transfer capability requires an investigation on their convection heat transfer behavior. Results of the previous tests indicate that the convection heat transfer coefficient follows the Reynolds number; i.e., the convection heat transfer coefficient will have a higher value in the case of turbulent flow and also, the heat transfer coefficient will be increased by increasing the Reynolds number. Increased shear stress in the wall, reduced thickness of the boundary layer, and delayed agglomeration of the nanoparticles are among the effective factors in increasing the heat transfer coefficient in the turbulent flow compared to laminar flow. Therefore, in this research, the convection heat transfer coefficient (h) of the base fluid and CRC-FGQDs nanofluid samples were measured at four concentrations as well as different Reynolds numbers in turbulent flow.

At first, in order to exhibit the precision and accuracy of the data obtained from measuring the convective heat transfer coefficient, the obtained data were validated through comparing with Gnielinski model. The Gnielinski equation for the turbulent flow is as Eq. (4) [34].
(4)
Nu =
f
8
( Re - 1000 ) Pr
1 + 12.7
f
8
0.5
(
pr
2
3
- 1 )

In Eq. (4), Prandtl number and parameter f are calculated through Eqs. (5) and (6). Where μ, Cp and K are viscosity, heat capacity and thermal conductivity, respectively.
(5)
Pr =
μ
C
P
K
(6)
f =
1
( 1.82
log
10
Re - 1.64 )
2
f

The process of validation was performed using the data of the deionized water’s convective heat transfer coefficient, the results of which have been shown in Fig. 8 . As it can be seen, there is a good relationship between the experimental data and the Gnielinski model’s data; thus, it can be concluded that it would be possible to measure the convective heat transfer coefficient of various fluids using this set-up, and the obtained data would be of acceptable precision and accuracy.

The results of convection heat transfer coefficient measurement of base fluid and CRC-FGQDs nanofluid samples are shown in Fig. 9 . As can be seen, the variation trend of h relative to the Reynolds number was ascending in all the samples. Besides, the addition of the nanoparticles to the base fluid led to the improved h, which was true for all the samples. Further, the results demonstrated that by increasing the concentration of the nanoparticles, the growth rate of the convection heat transfer coefficient has reduced. As mentioned earlier, this can be due to agglomeration of the particles at higher concentrations. Because, the number of the FGQD nanoparticles has drastically increased per unit volume by a slight increase in concentration due to their very small size. Thus, it can be concluded that the nanofluids containing GQDs have excellent stability and better performance at low concentrations.

According to Fig. 9, the 100-ppm sample had a better function than the base fluid. Table 3 shows the heat transfer behavior of the 100-ppm nanofluid at different Reynolds numbers; accordingly, the maximum increase in h compared to the base fluid was 17.2% at the Reynolds number of 4782. Furthermore, the results of the CRC-FGQDs nanofluid at 100ppm concentration was compared with Gnielinski model, according to the other researchers [39]. Kalbasi and Saeedi [40] simulated convection heat transfer of the copper oxide nanoparticles-containing nanofluids in a straight tube; subsequently, they compared their results with those of Gnielinski’s model. Moreover, Yarmand et al. [41] simulated the heat transfer ability using nanofluids in a rectangular pipe and, then, compared the obtained results with those of Gnielinski’s model. The Nusselt number for laboratory data was calculated using Eq. (7). Where h is the convection heat transfer coefficient of the nanofluid (based on Table 3); D denotes the internal diameter of the copper tube in the set-up, equal to 11.4mm; and K represents the thermal conductivity coefficient of the nanofluid at ambient temperature, equal to 0.387W/m·K based on Table 2.
(7)
Nu =
hD
K

Also, the Eq. (4) was used to calculate the Nusselt number based on Gnielinski model. The Prandtl number for the nanofluid was calculated to be 30 using Eq. (5) and according to its physical properties. Results are depicted in Fig. 10 . As can be seen, a very good relationship exists between experimental results and Gnielinski model.

Regarding the measurement results of the nanofluids’ thermal conductivity and convective heat transfer coefficients in the present research, it was observed that in different CRC-FGQDs samples, the improvement rate of the convective heat transfer coefficient was higher than that of the thermal conductivity coefficient. Apropos of this point, it can be said that although different factors influence the improvement rate of the nanofluids’ heat transferability, two phenomena of nanoparticles clusterization and Brownian motion are of significant importance in this regard. Since the clustering phenomenon is directly related to the concentration of nanoparticles, the use of graphene quantum dot nanoparticles with very small size (1–3nm) and very low concentration (100–1000ppm) has caused a relatively slight increase (5.2–7.1%) in the CRC-FGQDs nanofluid’s thermal conductivity coefficient. On the other hand, it should be noted that the Brownian motion, which directly affects the convective heat transfer coefficient, is increased significantly with reduction in the particle size. In fact, in the present research, the use of very small graphene quantum dot nanoparticles has resulted in a significant increase in the Brownian motion and, consequently, a favorable increase in the convection heat transfer coefficient of nanofluid (up to 17.2%).

The main goal of this research was to introduce nanofluids with high stability, appropriate heat transfer capability accompanied by environmentally friendly and cost-effective characteristics which leads to energy conservation. In fact, due to the possible environmental risks resulted from the use of metal and metal oxide nanoparticles for the synthesis of heat transfer nanofluids, biocompatible GQDs are considered in the present research. The GQDs were synthesized using citric acid and urea and then, thermal treatment process has been performed on GQDs in order to increase their specific surface area and activate their surface. Then, the CRC-FGQDs nanofluid samples were prepared at four different concentrations. Results indicated excellent stability of the nanofluids; so that, no change and deposition occurred in the samples after 6months. Further, thermal conductivity (k) and convection heat transfer coefficient (h) of the samples were studied; the results indicated 5–7% and 1–17% improvement of k and h for different samples in different conditions, respectively. On the other hand, the results showed that the synthesized FGQDs had better stability and performance at lower concentrations (100–500ppm) due to their small size, leading to production of cost-effective nanomaterials for application in nanofluids field.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Tarbiat Modares University and especially the Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI) of Iran for providing equipment to doing researches.
